enrichment.short_description: Qwilr helps sales and agency teams turn proposals, quotes, and contracts into interactive web pages with e-signatures and payments.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Qwilr is a proposal, quote, and sales document platform founded in Sydney, Australia in 2014 by Dylan Baskind and Mark Tanner. Instead of sending a static PDF or Word file, Qwilr lets sellers build proposals, quotes, and contracts as interactive, mobile-friendly web pages that buyers can view, sign, and pay from directly in the browser, without ever opening an attachment or installing software.

The core idea is a block-based page editor: users assemble a document from modular blocks such as text, images, embedded video, dynamic pricing tables, ROI calculators, and calendar links, then publish it as a shareable link rather than an attachment. This gives sales and agency teams a way to make pitches feel more like a polished landing page than a plain document, while still keeping the underlying legal and pricing content structured and reusable across the team.

Qwilr has grown into an established player in the proposal software category, offering three paid subscription tiers, Starter, Growth, and Scale, each billed per user with rising seat minimums as the tier increases, and a 14-day free trial that does not require a credit card, giving prospective customers a low-friction way to test the editor before committing.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Qwilr Is For
paragraphs: Qwilr is aimed squarely at B2B sales teams that send a steady volume of proposals, quotes, or statements of work and want every one of them on-brand without a designer re-building a deck each time. Reusable, templated pages let reps assemble a professional-looking document quickly while keeping pricing, legal language, and terms consistent across the whole team, rather than relying on copy-pasted slide decks.

It is also a strong fit for agencies, marketing shops, design studios, and consultancies that pitch new business regularly and need their proposals to double as a demonstration of their own creative or digital capability. An interactive, media-rich page that embeds video, case studies, and an itemized scope of work can differentiate a pitch in a way a static file simply cannot.

Revenue and sales operations teams already standardized on HubSpot or Salesforce are another good match, since Qwilr's integrations let a proposal be generated, tracked, and e-signed without leaving the CRM record, and engagement analytics feed back into the deal pipeline to show which sections buyers actually read, how long they spent on pricing, and whether the document was forwarded internally to other stakeholders.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Should Avoid It
paragraphs: Solo freelancers, very small businesses, or anyone sending only the occasional quote may find Qwilr more tool than they need. There is no free plan, pricing is per seat, and the Growth and Scale tiers carry five- and ten-seat minimums, which pushes the effective entry cost above simpler invoicing or e-signature-only tools that charge per document instead of per user.

Teams whose workflow depends on print-first or offline PDF documents, or that need to serve languages requiring right-to-left text, are also a weaker fit, since Qwilr pages are built for on-screen, English-first viewing and do not natively support right-to-left layout, which can limit usability for some international audiences.

Organizations that need heavyweight configure-price-quote logic, multi-party contract redlining, or complex multi-stage approval workflows may outgrow what Qwilr offers out of the box; those needs are usually better served by a dedicated CPQ or contract lifecycle management product, with Qwilr used alongside it purely for the buyer-facing presentation and signing layer rather than as the system of record for pricing logic.

long_form_blocks.heading: Main Features
paragraphs: At its core, Qwilr offers a block-based page builder for assembling proposals, quotes, and contracts from text, images, embedded video, dynamic pricing tables, and interactive calculators, all published as a single responsive web page instead of a file attachment. A library of templates helps teams start from an existing layout rather than a blank page.

Built-in e-signature lets buyers accept and sign directly on the page, and QwilrPay allows payment collection at the point of acceptance, combining the proposal, contract, and transaction into one flow; per-page and per-section analytics show how long a recipient spent on each part of the document, when it was reopened, and whether it was shared internally with other decision-makers.

Qwilr connects to common revenue tools including HubSpot, Salesforce, Zoho, Pipedrive, Quickbooks, Stripe, Slack, and Zapier, plus an API for custom workflows. Higher tiers add conditional content blocks that show or hide sections based on buyer choices, an AI-assisted Smart Proposal Engine for drafting and prefilling documents, custom branding and fonts, identity verification, and team-level permissions for larger organizations.

long_form_blocks.heading: The Qwilr Experience
paragraphs: Building a proposal in Qwilr feels closer to using a lightweight website builder than a document editor: users drag in blocks, apply a template, and preview the page as it will actually render for the buyer, rather than working within the fixed page boundaries of a traditional word processor.

On the recipient's side, pages load like any modern website, so buyers can scroll, watch embedded video, expand pricing options, and sign or pay on mobile or desktop without downloading anything or creating an account, and the sender is notified in near real time as that engagement happens.

Support and account-level attention scale with plan: Starter customers get self-serve access to the editor, e-signature, and analytics, Growth adds a dedicated customer success manager and enhanced reporting, and Scale adds chat support, an account manager, and enhanced API assistance, reflecting Qwilr's per-seat, tiered pricing structure and its focus on larger sales organizations at the top end.

features.title: Interactive page builder
features.description: A block-based editor lets teams assemble proposals from text, images, embedded video, pricing tables, and calculators, publishing the result as a web page instead of a file.

features.title: Templates
features.description: A library of reusable proposal and quote templates helps teams standardize layout and branding while speeding up document creation.

features.title: E-signature
features.description: Buyers can review and sign a proposal or contract directly on the page, without downloading a separate document or third-party tool.

features.title: QwilrPay
features.description: Payment collection is built into the page itself, so a buyer can accept a proposal and pay in the same flow.

features.title: Engagement analytics
features.description: Per-page and per-section tracking shows how long a recipient viewed each part of a document, when it was reopened, and whether it was shared internally.

features.title: CRM integrations
features.description: Native connections to HubSpot, Salesforce, Zoho, and Pipedrive let proposals be generated and tracked without leaving the CRM record.

features.title: Conditional content
features.description: Higher-tier plans allow blocks or sections to show or hide based on choices the buyer makes elsewhere in the page.

features.title: Smart Proposal Engine (AI Prefill)
features.description: An AI-assisted drafting feature on the Scale plan helps prefill proposal content to speed up document creation.

features.title: API access
features.description: An API is available on every plan for teams that want to build custom workflows or connect Qwilr to other internal systems.

features.title: Custom branding and permissions
features.description: Growth and Scale plans add custom fonts and branding, identity verification, and team-level permissions for larger organizations.

pros: Turns proposals and quotes into interactive, on-brand web pages instead of static files
Combines e-signature and payment collection in a single buyer-facing flow
Detailed engagement analytics show exactly how recipients interact with a document
Native integrations with major CRMs like HubSpot and Salesforce
14-day free trial with no credit card required to test the editor
cons: No free plan, and pricing is per seat rather than per document
Growth and Scale tiers require five and ten seats minimum, raising the entry cost for small teams
Interface and pages are English-first and do not support right-to-left languages
Advanced features like conditional content and the Smart Proposal Engine are reserved for higher tiers
Not designed for heavy configure-price-quote logic or complex multi-party contract redlining

faqs.question: What is Qwilr used for?
faqs.answer: Qwilr is used to create proposals, quotes, and sales contracts as interactive web pages that buyers can view, sign, and pay for online instead of receiving a static PDF or Word document.

faqs.question: Does Qwilr have a free plan?
faqs.answer: No, Qwilr does not offer a permanent free plan. It offers a 14-day free trial on the Starter tier that does not require a credit card, after which a paid subscription is required.

faqs.question: How much does Qwilr cost?
faqs.answer: Qwilr's Starter plan starts at $35 per user per month when billed annually, with Growth and Scale tiers priced higher and requiring five and ten user seats minimum respectively.

faqs.question: Does Qwilr support e-signatures and payments?
faqs.answer: Yes. Qwilr includes built-in e-signature so buyers can accept documents on the page, and QwilrPay allows payment collection at the point of acceptance.

faqs.question: Which tools does Qwilr integrate with?
faqs.answer: Qwilr integrates with HubSpot, Salesforce, Zoho, Pipedrive, Quickbooks, Stripe, Slack, and Zapier, and also offers an API for custom integrations.

use_cases.title: Sending sales proposals
use_cases.description: B2B sales reps build branded, interactive proposals with embedded pricing and video, then track how prospects engage with them.

use_cases.title: Agency new-business pitches
use_cases.description: Marketing and creative agencies use interactive pages with case studies and portfolio media to make new-business pitches more compelling.

use_cases.title: Quotes and contracts with e-signature
use_cases.description: Teams combine a price quote, contract terms, and an e-signature step into a single page so a buyer can review and accept in one sitting.

use_cases.title: CRM-embedded proposal workflows
use_cases.description: Sales operations teams generate and track Qwilr pages directly from HubSpot or Salesforce records, keeping proposal activity tied to the deal.

pricing_plans.billing_cycle: Annual (monthly billing also available at a higher rate)

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Qwilr review: pricing, features, pros and cons of the interactive proposal, quote, and e-signature software for sales and agency teams.

enrichment.short_description: Cloud platform for deploying apps, databases, and background jobs from GitHub with usage-based pricing.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Railway is a cloud infrastructure platform that lets developers deploy applications, databases, and background services without managing servers directly. Founded in 2020 and headquartered in San Francisco, California, the company built its platform around a simple premise: connect a GitHub repository, let Railway detect the language and build tooling automatically, and push to a live URL within minutes. It has become a frequently cited alternative to Heroku after that platform scaled back its free tier, and it is often mentioned alongside Render and Fly.io as part of a newer wave of developer-first infrastructure providers.

Under the hood, Railway uses its own build system, Nixpacks, to inspect a repository and generate a container image without requiring a Dockerfile, though teams that prefer full control can still supply one. Projects are organized into workspaces, environments, and services, letting a single codebase support isolated staging and production copies with their own databases and environment variables. Managed Postgres, MySQL, Redis, MongoDB, and ClickHouse instances can be added from the same dashboard used to deploy application code.

Pricing is usage-based rather than flat-rate: every plan carries a minimum monthly commitment that also acts as a resource credit, and CPU, memory, storage, and network egress are metered on top of that baseline. This model appeals to teams that want to avoid overpaying for idle capacity, though it also means monthly bills can vary with traffic and usage patterns rather than staying fixed. Because of this, teams evaluating Railway typically model a few weeks of expected traffic before deciding whether the usage-based approach will be cheaper than a fixed-instance provider for their specific workload.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Railway Is For
paragraphs: Railway is best suited to individual developers, indie hackers, and small engineering teams who want to ship a working application quickly without hiring dedicated infrastructure staff. Anyone comfortable with Git-based workflows but unwilling to hand-configure servers, load balancers, or container orchestration will find Railway's push-to-deploy experience a natural fit.

Startups and early-stage SaaS teams that have outgrown a free-tier PaaS but are not yet ready for a full Kubernetes setup also fit the platform well. Railway's per-environment isolation makes it straightforward to spin up staging copies of a production stack, which is valuable for teams iterating quickly on customer-facing features.

Side-project builders and students prototyping full-stack apps benefit from the trial credit and template marketplace, which let them get a database-backed application online without prior deployment experience. Freelancers and small agencies managing several client projects also use Railway to keep each engagement in its own workspace with separate billing visibility, making it easier to track costs and access per client without maintaining separate cloud accounts.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Should Avoid It
paragraphs: Large enterprises with strict multi-region redundancy, custom networking, or complex compliance requirements may find Railway's feature set narrower than a self-managed Kubernetes cluster or a hyperscale cloud provider. Teams that need fine-grained control over infrastructure topology are likely to hit the platform's ceiling sooner than they would on a lower-level cloud service.

Organizations running compute-intensive workloads with high, sustained CPU or memory usage should model costs carefully before committing, since usage-based billing can become more expensive than a reserved-instance or self-hosted setup at scale. Teams with unpredictable traffic spikes may also see monthly bills fluctuate in ways that complicate budgeting.

Because Railway no longer offers an indefinite free tier, hobbyists who need to run a project at zero ongoing cost for an extended period may prefer a platform with a permanent free plan. Companies with existing investment in Kubernetes manifests or a different PaaS may also find the migration effort outweighs the benefits Railway offers, particularly if their deployment pipelines are already tightly coupled to a specific provider's tooling.

long_form_blocks.heading: Main Features
paragraphs: At its core, Railway automates the path from source code to a running service. Pushing to a connected GitHub branch triggers a build through Nixpacks or a supplied Dockerfile, and the resulting deployment gets a public URL with automatic TLS. The CLI mirrors this workflow for local development, letting developers deploy from a directory without touching the web dashboard.

The platform bundles managed database services, private networking between services within the same project, and horizontal or vertical scaling controls so a service can add replicas or adjust its resource allocation as load changes. A template marketplace offers one-click starting points for common stacks, from web frameworks to self-hosted open-source tools.

Observability is built into the dashboard rather than bolted on: logs, CPU and memory metrics, and deployment history are all visible per service, which helps teams diagnose issues without wiring up a separate monitoring stack. Environment variables, secrets, and scheduled jobs round out the feature set for teams running more than a single web process, and shared variable references let multiple services stay in sync without duplicating configuration.

long_form_blocks.heading: The Railway Experience
paragraphs: Day-to-day, Railway's reputation rests on speed and simplicity: most users describe going from a GitHub repository to a live, database-backed application in a matter of minutes, with little manual configuration required. The dashboard favors visual clarity over dense settings screens, and the CLI is often praised for mirroring what's possible in the browser.

Documentation covers common deployment scenarios, environment setup, and the pricing model in reasonable depth, and an active community supplements official support for troubleshooting and pattern-sharing. Paid plans add longer log retention and team collaboration features, while enterprise customers get contractual SLAs and dedicated support channels.

The tradeoff for that simplicity is less visibility into exactly how usage translates into cost until a bill arrives, a common complaint about usage-based platforms in general. Overall, developers who value shipping quickly over infrastructure control tend to rate the experience highly, while teams needing deep customization sometimes graduate to a lower-level cloud provider once they scale, treating Railway as the platform that got their product to market quickly in the first place.

features.title: GitHub-Connected Deployments
features.description: Push-to-deploy from a connected repository, with automatic builds triggered on every commit to a chosen branch.

features.title: Nixpacks Auto-Detection
features.description: Automatically detects a project's language and dependencies to build a container image without requiring a handwritten Dockerfile.

features.title: Managed Databases
features.description: One-click Postgres, MySQL, Redis, MongoDB, and ClickHouse instances provisioned alongside application services.

features.title: Private Networking
features.description: Services within a project communicate over an internal private network, reducing exposed surface area and egress costs.

features.title: Horizontal and Vertical Scaling
features.description: Add replicas or adjust CPU and memory allocation per service as traffic and load change.

features.title: Built-In Observability
features.description: Per-service logs, resource metrics, and deployment history are available directly in the dashboard.

features.title: Environments and Workspaces
features.description: Isolate staging, production, and preview copies of a project, each with its own variables and databases.

features.title: Template Marketplace
features.description: One-click templates for popular frameworks and self-hosted tools speed up first deployments.

features.title: Railway CLI
features.description: Deploy, view logs, and manage environment variables from the terminal without leaving a local workflow.

features.title: Usage-Based Billing Dashboard
features.description: Real-time visibility into per-service resource consumption that feeds directly into the monthly bill.

pros: Very fast path from a GitHub repository to a live, database-backed deployment
Usage-based pricing avoids paying for a fixed instance size that sits idle
Managed databases and a template marketplace remove common setup friction
Private networking and per-environment isolation are included without extra configuration
CLI and dashboard are widely regarded as clean and easy to learn
cons: No indefinite free plan; ongoing use always requires a paid Hobby or Pro subscription
Usage-based costs can be harder to predict than a flat monthly fee, especially under variable traffic
Less low-level infrastructure control than Kubernetes or a hyperscale cloud provider
Sustained, high-resource workloads may end up costing more than a reserved-capacity alternative
Advanced compliance and support features are reserved for the custom-priced Enterprise tier

faqs.question: Is Railway free to use?
faqs.answer: Railway offers a one-time trial credit (around $5) that doesn't require a credit card, but ongoing usage requires a paid Hobby or Pro plan once the credit is used, since there is no longer a permanent free tier.

faqs.question: What can I deploy on Railway?
faqs.answer: Railway supports web applications, APIs, background workers, cron-style jobs, and managed databases like Postgres, MySQL, Redis, and MongoDB, typically deployed straight from a connected GitHub repository or a Docker image.

faqs.question: How does Railway's pricing actually work?
faqs.answer: Each plan sets a minimum monthly spend that also acts as a resource credit; CPU, memory, storage, and network egress are then metered and billed on top of that baseline as usage grows.

faqs.question: Is Railway a good alternative to Heroku?
faqs.answer: Many teams that left Heroku after its pricing and free-tier changes use Railway for a similar push-to-deploy experience, though the two platforms differ in pricing structure and add-on ecosystems.

faqs.question: Does Railway support team collaboration?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Pro plan adds per-seat team features, longer log retention, and higher resource limits, while the Enterprise tier adds SSO, contractual SLAs, and dedicated support.

use_cases.title: Shipping a Full-Stack Web App from GitHub
use_cases.description: Connect a repository, let Nixpacks detect the framework, and get a live URL with a managed Postgres database attached, without writing deployment scripts.
use_cases.audience: Indie developers and small product teams

use_cases.title: Hosting a Database for a Side Project or Prototype
use_cases.description: Provision a managed Postgres, MySQL, Redis, or MongoDB instance in a few clicks to back a weekend project or proof of concept.

use_cases.title: Running Background Workers and Scheduled Jobs
use_cases.description: Deploy long-running workers or cron-style scheduled tasks alongside a primary web service within the same project and private network.

use_cases.title: Managing Staging and Production Environments
use_cases.description: Use isolated environments within a single project to test changes safely before promoting them to a production deployment.

Resource usage deducted from the trial credit
Upgrade to Hobby or Pro once the credit is used
$5 of resource usage included per month
Usage beyond the included credit billed on top
Intended for solo developers and personal projects
$20 of resource usage included per seat
Higher resource limits and longer log retention
Team collaboration features for production workloads

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Railway is a usage-based cloud platform for deploying apps, databases, and background jobs straight from GitHub.

enrichment.short_description: Ramp is a free corporate card and spend management platform combining expenses, bill pay, procurement, and accounting automation.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (paid Plus plan from $15/user/month plus a platform fee)
enrichment.headquarters: New York City, New York, USA

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Ramp is a financial technology company founded in 2019 and headquartered in New York City. The company built its business around a corporate charge card that carries no annual fee, no interest, and no card-replacement fees for customers on its free plan, monetizing primarily through interchange revenue rather than subscription fees. Over time Ramp expanded from a single card product into a broader spend management suite.

Today the platform bundles corporate cards, expense management, accounts payable and bill pay, procurement, business travel booking, and accounting automation into one system. Finance teams can issue unlimited virtual and physical cards with built-in spend controls, automatically match receipts submitted by SMS, email, Slack, or the mobile app, and sync coded transactions to accounting and ERP systems such as QuickBooks, Xero, Sage Intacct, and NetSuite.

Ramp offers a genuinely free tier alongside paid Plus and Enterprise plans that unlock deeper automation, additional integrations, and premium support. This freemium structure lets very small teams run their entire card and expense workflow at no monthly cost, while growing organizations can pay for more advanced approval logic, budgeting, and ERP connectivity as their needs mature.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Ramp Is For
paragraphs: Ramp is built for finance and operations teams at startups and small-to-midsize businesses that want to consolidate multiple point tools for cards, expenses, and payables into a single dashboard. Companies that are actively trying to cut manual bookkeeping work, close their books faster, and reduce time spent chasing receipts tend to be a strong fit, since much of the platform's value comes from automating those repetitive tasks.

It also suits organizations that already use, or plan to adopt, mainstream accounting or ERP software, since Ramp's integrations with tools like QuickBooks, Xero, Sage Intacct, and NetSuite are central to how coded transactions flow into the general ledger. Businesses with multiple departments or cost centers that need real-time budget visibility and configurable approval chains benefit from Ramp's role-based controls and reporting.

Finally, teams that want a single vendor for corporate cards, bill pay, procurement, and business travel rather than stitching together separate tools are natural candidates. Founders and controllers who value a modern, self-serve interface over long implementation cycles typically find the onboarding experience approachable compared with legacy expense and procurement software.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Should Avoid It
paragraphs: Very early-stage or pre-revenue companies without an established U.S. business banking history may find card underwriting and credit limits more restrictive than expected, since card approval and limits are tied to the business's financial profile. Companies whose operations are heavily international, or that need extensive local-currency card issuance and multi-entity support outside Ramp's supported countries, may find coverage more limited than global-first competitors.

Organizations that primarily need invoicing and accounts receivable tools should also look elsewhere, since Ramp is focused on the spend and payables side of finance rather than billing customers or collecting revenue. Businesses that are satisfied with an existing, deeply customized combination of card, expense, and ERP tools may not see enough incremental benefit to justify a migration, particularly if they rely on integrations or workflows Ramp does not yet support.

Teams that want unlimited phone-based or highly white-glove support without moving to a paid plan may also be frustrated, since the richest support and account management experience is generally reserved for Plus and Enterprise customers. Companies uncomfortable with a card provider that earns revenue from interchange rather than subscription fees, or that require pricing structures entirely different from Ramp's free-plus-paid-tier model, should evaluate alternatives before committing.

long_form_blocks.heading: Main Features
paragraphs: At its core, Ramp provides corporate charge cards with configurable spend limits and controls, issued as unlimited physical and virtual cards without the fees typically associated with traditional business credit cards. Expense management is built around automatic receipt capture and matching, so employees can submit expenses through SMS, email, Slack, or the mobile app instead of manual expense reports.

The accounts payable and bill pay module uses optical character recognition to extract invoice data, route bills through configurable approval workflows, and schedule vendor payments, reducing manual data entry for finance teams. Accounting automation auto-codes transactions and syncs them to connected accounting or ERP platforms, while procurement tools add purchase-request workflows and vendor management for organizations on paid plans.

Ramp also includes integrated business travel booking for flights and hotels tied to company spend policy, a treasury offering for holding and moving business cash, real-time reporting and budget dashboards for cross-department visibility, and AI-driven capabilities the company markets under its Ramp Intelligence branding for tasks like anomaly detection and policy checks. Custom approval workflows and role-based permissions round out the platform's administrative controls.

long_form_blocks.heading: The Ramp Experience
paragraphs: Day to day, Ramp is generally described as a modern, self-serve platform: signing up and issuing cards can happen quickly, and the web dashboard and mobile app share a consistent, relatively uncluttered design compared with older finance software. Admins can set spend policies, review transactions, and approve bills from either the browser or the mobile app, which also supports on-the-go reimbursement submission with receipt photos and mileage tracking.

For employees, the experience is centered on avoiding manual expense reports: card swipes trigger automatic prompts for receipts and memos through email, Slack, or text message, and most routine transactions require little additional effort once policies are configured. Approvals for cards, bills, reimbursements, and limit changes can be handled from a mobile inbox, which is useful for managers who need to respond quickly while away from a desk.

Support and account experience differs by plan: free-tier customers get self-serve resources and standard support channels, while Plus and Enterprise customers gain access to priority support and, at the top tier, dedicated account management and white-glove implementation help. Overall, the platform is positioned around reducing the operational overhead of running corporate cards, payables, and expense processes rather than being the cheapest raw card product on the market.

features.description: Unlimited physical and virtual cards with configurable spend limits and controls, issued with no annual fee or interest on the free plan.

features.title: Expense Management
features.description: Automatic receipt capture and matching through SMS, email, Slack, or the mobile app, cutting down on manual expense reports.

features.title: Accounts Payable & Bill Pay
features.description: OCR-based invoice processing, configurable approval workflows, and scheduled vendor payments to automate accounts payable.

features.title: Accounting Automation
features.description: Auto-coded transactions that sync to connected accounting and ERP systems such as QuickBooks, Xero, Sage Intacct, and NetSuite.

features.title: Procurement
features.description: Purchase-request workflows and vendor management tools available as an add-on for teams on paid plans.

features.title: Business Travel
features.description: Integrated flight and hotel booking tied to company spend policy, with trip-level expense tracking.

features.title: Ramp Treasury
features.description: Business cash management for holding and moving funds alongside card and payables activity.

features.title: Real-Time Reporting & Budgets
features.description: Dashboards for cross-department spend visibility, budget tracking, and finance reporting.

features.title: Ramp Intelligence (AI)
features.description: AI-driven policy checks, anomaly detection, and auto-classification aimed at reducing manual review work.

features.title: Custom Approval Workflows & Roles
features.description: Configurable multi-level approval chains and role-based permissions for admins, managers, and employees.

pros: Free core plan covers unlimited corporate cards and expense management with no monthly fee
Deep automation around receipt matching and expense coding reduces manual finance work
All-in-one suite covering cards, AP, procurement, travel, and accounting reduces the need for separate tools
Strong integrations with mainstream accounting and ERP systems like QuickBooks, Xero, Sage Intacct, and NetSuite
Modern, well-regarded web dashboard and mobile app for both admins and employees
cons: Advanced automation, procurement, and premium integrations require upgrading to the paid Plus or Enterprise plan
Card underwriting and limits depend on business financial history, which can be restrictive for pre-revenue companies
International coverage for local-currency cards and multi-entity support is more limited than some global competitors
No dedicated invoicing or accounts receivable functionality, since the platform is focused on spend and payables
A foreign transaction fee applies to card purchases made outside the free plan's supported currency terms

faqs.question: Is Ramp free to use?
faqs.answer: Ramp offers a free plan that includes unlimited corporate cards and core expense management with no monthly fee; the company earns revenue primarily from card interchange rather than charging for the base plan.

faqs.question: What is included in Ramp's free plan?
faqs.answer: The free plan includes unlimited virtual and physical corporate cards, expense management with receipt matching, basic accounts payable, treasury features, and fundamental accounting automation.

faqs.question: Does Ramp integrate with accounting software?
faqs.answer: Yes, Ramp integrates with accounting and ERP platforms including QuickBooks, Xero, Sage Intacct, and NetSuite, with some deeper integrations available on paid plans.

faqs.question: Is there a Ramp mobile app?
faqs.answer: Yes, Ramp has a mobile app for iOS and Android that supports card payments, receipt submission, mileage tracking, approvals, and travel booking.

faqs.question: How does Ramp make money if the card is free?
faqs.answer: Ramp generates revenue mainly from interchange fees on card transactions, supplemented by subscription revenue from its paid Plus and Enterprise plans and certain add-ons.

use_cases.title: Startup card and expense management
use_cases.description: Early-stage companies issue employee cards, set spend limits, and automate receipt collection without adopting a paid expense tool.

use_cases.title: Accounts payable automation
use_cases.description: Mid-market finance teams use OCR-based invoice capture and approval workflows to process vendor bills and payments faster.

use_cases.title: Cross-department budget control
use_cases.description: Organizations with multiple teams track real-time spend against budgets and enforce role-based approval policies.

use_cases.title: Policy-compliant business travel
use_cases.description: Companies book flights and hotels through Ramp Travel so bookings automatically follow spend policy and sync to expense records.

Expanded integrations including NetSuite and Sage Intacct

White-glove support with dedicated account managers

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Ramp offers free corporate cards and an all-in-one spend management platform: expenses, bill pay, procurement, and accounting.

enrichment.short_description: Recruitee (Tellent Recruitee) is a cloud-based ATS with collaborative pipelines, a careers site builder, and AI-assisted screening for growing teams.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Recruitee, now operating under the Tellent group as Tellent Recruitee, is a cloud-based applicant tracking system built to help small and mid-sized businesses run their hiring process from one place. Founded in 2015 in Amsterdam, the Netherlands, it grew from an early-stage hiring tool into a broader recruitment platform aimed at teams that need structure and collaboration without the complexity of a full enterprise HR suite. Its Amsterdam roots are still reflected in the product, which leans toward European hiring norms and multilingual careers pages alongside its English-first interface.

The platform centers on a visual hiring pipeline where recruiters can move candidates through customizable stages, tag colleagues for feedback, and automate repetitive steps such as interview scheduling and follow-up emails. A branded careers page, called CareersHub, lets companies publish job openings and collect applications directly, without needing separate web development or a dedicated careers site build. Recruiters can also share individual candidate profiles internally with a link, which keeps hiring managers looped in without requiring every stakeholder to log into the full system.

Since becoming part of Tellent, Recruitee has added AI-assisted job writing, candidate screening, and matching tools on top of its existing collaboration and reporting features. This positions it as a middle-ground option between free spreadsheet-based hiring trackers and much larger, higher-cost enterprise talent acquisition platforms aimed at large HR departments. Tellent also sells complementary HR products alongside Recruitee, which gives existing customers a path to add broader people-management tools later without switching vendors entirely.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Recruitee Is For
paragraphs: Recruitee is generally aimed at small and mid-sized companies that are hiring often enough to outgrow email threads and spreadsheets but do not have a dedicated recruiting operations team. Startups scaling headcount, agencies running multiple client searches, and growing HR teams building their first real hiring process are common fits for the platform.

Hiring managers who need visibility without living inside the tool also benefit, since Recruitee is built around shared pipelines, comment threads, and scorecards that let non-recruiters weigh in on candidates quickly. Companies that want a self-serve careers page and simple job board distribution without hiring a developer tend to find the setup approachable.

Teams already committed to Slack, common calendar tools, and standard job boards will likely get the most value, since Recruitee's integrations and workflow automations are built around those everyday tools rather than niche or highly specialized recruiting stacks. Companies running employee referral programs may also appreciate the built-in referrals module, which gives internal staff a simple way to submit candidates without a separate system.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Should Avoid It
paragraphs: Very large enterprises with complex, multi-country compliance needs, deep HRIS integration requirements, or highly customized approval chains may find Recruitee's structure more limiting than a large enterprise-grade suite designed specifically for that scale. Organizations that need extensive configurability across many business units should evaluate whether Recruitee's tiers cover their governance needs.

Companies that hire only occasionally, such as very small businesses making one or two hires a year, may find a paid ATS unnecessary and could be better served by free or lower-cost alternatives until hiring volume grows enough to justify a dedicated tool.

Teams that need pricing certainty upfront may also want to look elsewhere first, since Recruitee's current plans are quoted based on employee count and billing period rather than published flat rates, which means budgeting requires a sales conversation rather than a simple self-serve signup.

long_form_blocks.heading: Main Features
paragraphs: At its core, Recruitee provides a customizable hiring pipeline, candidate database, and collaborative hiring tools that let recruiters and hiring managers work from a shared view of every open role. Pipelines can be tailored per role, and bulk actions help manage high volumes of applicants without manual repetition.

The CareersHub careers site and job board distribution tools let companies publish openings across multiple channels from a single posting, while an employee referrals module helps surface internal candidate leads. Interview scheduling tools sync with common calendar systems to reduce the back-and-forth of coordinating hiring panels.

AI-assisted features, including a writing assistant for job descriptions and screening and matching assistants for surfacing relevant candidates from an existing database, are layered onto the higher plans. Reporting and analytics tools track pipeline health and hiring metrics, with more advanced custom reporting available on upper tiers alongside integrations, single sign-on, and requisition approval workflows for larger teams. A browser extension is also available for sourcing candidate details directly from professional networking sites into the pipeline.

long_form_blocks.heading: The Recruitee Experience
paragraphs: Using Recruitee day to day generally starts with setting up a careers site and job templates, then building out pipeline stages that match how a company actually interviews. Because the interface is built around drag-and-drop candidate cards and shared comment threads, most hiring managers can be onboarded with a short walkthrough rather than formal training.

Recruiters tend to spend their time inside candidate profiles and pipeline views, using tags, scorecards, and automated emails to keep processes moving without manually re-entering information. The mobile-friendly interface and browser extension for sourcing candidates from professional networks are frequently mentioned as conveniences that reduce manual data entry.

Overall, the experience is closer to a modern collaboration tool than a legacy enterprise system, which is consistent with Recruitee's original positioning for growing teams. Companies moving up from spreadsheets often describe the transition as straightforward, while teams with more complex compliance or approval needs may need to lean on the higher tiers or support resources to configure the platform to fit their process. Because pricing now requires a sales conversation rather than a self-serve checkout, prospective customers should expect an onboarding call before they can compare exact costs across tiers.

Built-in careers site and multi-channel job posting reduce the need for extra tools
AI writing, screening, and matching features are built directly into upper plans
Employee referrals and calendar-synced interview scheduling reduce manual coordination work
Free trial available with no credit card required to explore the platform
cons: Pricing is no longer published and requires a sales conversation based on employee count
Annual contracts appear to be the standard commitment, which limits short-term flexibility
Advanced AI and compliance features are reserved for higher tiers, adding cost for smaller teams

Deep enterprise-grade compliance and multi-country configuration may be more limited than dedicated large-enterprise HR suites

faqs.question: What is Recruitee used for?
faqs.answer: Recruitee is an applicant tracking system used to manage job postings, candidate pipelines, interview scheduling, and hiring collaboration for small and mid-sized companies.

faqs.question: Is Recruitee the same as Tellent?
faqs.answer: Recruitee operates as Tellent Recruitee, a product within the Tellent group, which also owns other HR software products; the Recruitee ATS itself continues to operate under that branding.

faqs.question: Does Recruitee offer a free trial?
faqs.answer: Yes, Recruitee offers a free trial that does not require a credit card, allowing teams to test the platform before committing to a paid plan.

faqs.question: How is Recruitee priced?
faqs.answer: Recruitee's plans are quoted based on the number of employees at your organization and your billing period, so exact pricing requires contacting sales rather than a published rate.

faqs.question: Does Recruitee include AI features?
faqs.answer: Yes, Recruitee includes AI-assisted job description writing on its entry plan, with AI candidate screening and matching assistants available on higher-tier plans.

use_cases.title: Replacing spreadsheet-based hiring
use_cases.description: Move candidate tracking, interview notes, and hiring status out of shared spreadsheets and into a single collaborative pipeline.
use_cases.audience: Growing small businesses hiring their first dedicated recruiter or HR hire

use_cases.title: Building a self-serve careers site
use_cases.description: Launch a branded careers page and distribute job postings across multiple channels without a separate web project.
use_cases.audience: Startups and SMBs without in-house web development resources

use_cases.title: Running multiple concurrent searches
use_cases.description: Manage several open roles at once with per-role pipelines, tagging, and collaborator comments across hiring teams.
use_cases.audience: Recruiting agencies and internal talent teams handling multiple simultaneous roles

use_cases.title: Scaling employee referrals
use_cases.description: Use the built-in referrals module to let employees submit candidates for open roles and track referral outcomes.
use_cases.audience: Mid-sized companies building an internal referral pipeline alongside external sourcing

enrichment.short_description: Recurly is a subscription billing and revenue management platform that automates recurring billing, payment orchestration, churn recovery, and revenue recognition.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Recurly is a subscription billing and revenue management platform founded in 2009 and headquartered in San Francisco, California. It was built specifically for companies whose revenue model depends on recurring billing rather than one-time transactions, and it has grown into one of the more established names in the subscription commerce space alongside competitors such as Chargebee and Zuora.

At its core, Recurly automates the mechanics of recurring billing: creating and managing subscription plans, calculating prorations for upgrades and downgrades, retrying failed payments through dunning management, and reconciling revenue across many payment gateways and methods. Beyond core billing, Recurly has expanded into adjacent products it markets separately - Engage for churn prevention and customer engagement, and RevRec for automated revenue recognition under multi-GAAP accounting rules.

Recurly positions itself less as a simple payment processor and more as end-to-end subscription infrastructure, offering its own APIs and client libraries for developers who want to build custom billing logic on top of the platform, alongside a hosted subscriber portal for teams that prefer configuration over code. Pricing is largely tied to the billing volume a business processes rather than flat per-seat fees, a structural difference worth understanding before evaluating the platform.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Recurly Is For
paragraphs: Recurly is aimed at subscription-based businesses - SaaS companies, streaming and media services, subscription boxes, and membership sites - that have moved past relying on a payment processor's native billing tools and need dedicated recurring-billing infrastructure. Its entry-level Starter plan, which includes a 90-day free trial, is built for smaller businesses processing roughly under $40,000 in monthly billings.

The platform's more advanced All-Access tier requires at least $1 million in annual billing volume, signaling that Recurly's deeper feature set - AI-assisted churn prevention, multicurrency support, payments orchestration across many gateways, and single sign-on - is really designed for growth-stage and mid-market subscription companies rather than early-stage startups.

Recurly also offers a dedicated bundle for Shopify-based subscription commerce, making it a reasonable fit for direct-to-consumer subscription-box or replenishment brands selling through Shopify. Finance and revenue teams at companies with complex, contract-driven revenue recognition needs may also consider Recurly specifically for its RevRec add-on rather than the billing core alone.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Should Avoid It
paragraphs: Very small or pre-revenue businesses that only need to charge a handful of customers a flat recurring fee are usually better served by a payment processor's built-in subscription tools or a lighter-weight billing product, since Recurly's percentage-of-volume pricing and setup overhead are hard to justify at very low billing volume.

Companies that need highly bespoke, non-standard billing logic - complex hybrid usage metering, for instance - may find that some competitors emphasize flexible metered billing more directly, and should compare specifically on that dimension before committing, since Recurly's core strength has traditionally been subscription and plan-based billing rather than granular usage metering.

Businesses that want fully transparent, self-serve pricing without a sales conversation may be frustrated by Recurly, since most tiers beyond Starter are quoted individually and require a minimum billing volume commitment; teams that prioritize pricing transparency over feature depth may prefer a vendor that publishes flat, non-negotiated tiers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Main Features
paragraphs: Recurly's core billing engine covers the standard recurring-revenue workflow: configurable subscription plans, trials, coupons and promotions, prorated upgrades and downgrades, and support for many payment methods across a large number of payment gateways, reducing the need to stitch together separate tools for each region or payment type.

Dunning and churn-recovery tooling is a central selling point - Recurly runs automated retry logic and multiple configurable dunning campaigns to recover failed payments, and its higher tiers layer on AI-assisted, predictive churn models that estimate which subscribers are at risk of canceling so a team can intervene with targeted offers.

For finance-heavy use cases, the RevRec add-on automates contract modifications, rules-driven revenue allocation, and multi-GAAP compliant revenue recognition with an audit trail, while the core platform also offers a hosted subscriber management portal, custom reporting, and REST APIs with official client libraries for languages including Ruby, Python, Node.js, Java, PHP, and .NET.

long_form_blocks.heading: The Recurly Experience
paragraphs: Setting up Recurly typically starts with defining subscription plans and connecting one or more payment gateways; the dashboard centers on subscriber accounts, invoices, and billing events, and most day-to-day billing operations - issuing credits, pausing a subscription, updating a payment method - can be handled without engineering involvement once initial configuration is in place.

Developers who need custom checkout flows, usage-based add-ons, or integrations with a broader billing stack rely on Recurly's REST API and webhooks, and the platform documents its client libraries and API versioning, including a defined support policy for older API versions, reasonably thoroughly for teams building on top of it.

Because pricing and onboarding for anything beyond the Starter plan tend to involve a sales conversation, the buying experience feels closer to enterprise software procurement than a self-serve SaaS signup - something to budget time for when evaluating Recurly against tools with instant self-serve pricing.

features.title: Subscription plan management
features.description: Configure flexible subscription plans, free trials, coupons, and promotions, with automatic proration for upgrades and downgrades.

features.title: Automated dunning and churn recovery
features.description: Recover failed payments with automated retry logic and multiple configurable dunning campaigns.

features.title: AI-assisted churn prediction
features.description: Higher tiers include a propensity-to-churn model that flags at-risk subscribers so teams can offer targeted retention incentives.

features.title: Multi-gateway payment orchestration
features.description: Connect to a broad range of payment gateways and support 10+ payment methods for global billing.

features.title: Multicurrency and global billing support
features.description: Bill subscribers in multiple currencies and support region-specific payment preferences.

features.title: Revenue recognition (RevRec)
features.description: An add-on product that automates contract modifications, rules-driven revenue allocation, and multi-GAAP compliant revenue recognition with an audit trail.

features.title: Hosted subscriber management portal
features.description: Give subscribers a self-serve portal to manage their own billing details, plans, and cancellations.

features.title: REST API and client libraries
features.description: Build custom billing workflows using Recurly's API, with official client libraries for several popular programming languages.

features.title: Custom reporting and analytics
features.description: Access reporting on subscription metrics such as billing volume, recovered revenue, and churn.

features.title: Shopify subscription commerce integration
features.description: A dedicated All-Access for Shopify bundle supports bundles, prepaid and gift subscriptions, and self-serve subscription management for Shopify stores.

pros: Purpose-built for recurring and subscription billing with deep dunning and churn-recovery tooling
Broad payment gateway and payment method coverage for global billing operations
Optional RevRec add-on handles complex, multi-GAAP revenue recognition
Official API client libraries across several popular programming languages
Established vendor with a long track record in the subscription billing space since 2009
cons: Pricing beyond the Starter plan is quoted individually rather than published, requiring a sales conversation
Percentage-of-billing-volume pricing can become expensive as revenue scales
The All-Access tier requires a $1 million+ annual billing volume minimum, out of reach for small businesses
AI-assisted churn prediction and other advanced features are gated behind higher tiers or add-ons
Less suited to businesses needing highly granular usage-based metering as their primary billing model

faqs.question: Does Recurly offer a free trial?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Starter plan includes a 90-day free trial before billing begins.

faqs.question: How does Recurly's pricing work?
faqs.answer: The Starter plan combines a flat monthly fee with a percentage of billing volume, while higher tiers such as All-Access are quoted individually based on billing volume and contract terms.

faqs.question: What payment gateways and methods does Recurly support?
faqs.answer: Recurly connects to a large number of payment gateways and supports 10+ payment methods, expanding to additional methods like ACH on higher tiers.

faqs.question: Does Recurly integrate with Shopify?
faqs.answer: Yes, Recurly offers an All-Access for Shopify bundle tailored to Shopify-based subscription commerce.

faqs.question: What is RevRec?
faqs.answer: RevRec is Recurly's revenue recognition add-on that automates contract modifications and rules-driven revenue allocation to help finance teams stay compliant with multi-GAAP standards.
