enrichment.short_description: Open-source in-memory data store used as a database, cache, and message broker, with managed cloud and enterprise offerings from Redis Ltd.
enrichment.starting_price: $5/month (Essentials plan); a free tier is also available
enrichment.headquarters: San Francisco, California, USA (additional offices including Tel Aviv, Israel)

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Redis is an open-source, in-memory data store that doubles as a database, cache, and message broker, prized for sub-millisecond read and write latency. Originally created by Salvatore Sanfilippo in 2009 to solve real-time scaling problems, it introduced a compact set of high-performance data structures - strings, hashes, lists, sets, and sorted sets - that made it easy to model application state directly in memory instead of translating everything into rows and columns.

The project is now stewarded commercially by Redis Ltd (formerly Redis Labs), headquartered in San Francisco with additional offices including Tel Aviv, while the core software remains freely downloadable and self-hostable. Redis Ltd sells managed and enterprise offerings, Redis Cloud and Redis Enterprise, that add clustering, active-active replication, and operational tooling on top of the open-source engine.

Over the years Redis has grown well beyond a simple key-value cache. Modules and built-in capabilities extend it into search, JSON document storage, time-series data, probabilistic data structures, and vector similarity search for AI applications, so many teams now run it as a multi-purpose real-time data platform rather than a single-purpose cache.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Redis Is For
paragraphs: Redis suits backend and platform engineering teams building applications where latency matters, such as product pages, feeds, and APIs that need to shave milliseconds off every request. Its data structures map naturally onto common problems like counters, rate limiters, leaderboards, and session state, so developers can implement these features directly instead of bolting them onto a relational database.

It is also a strong fit for teams building real-time and event-driven systems: chat and notification services using Pub/Sub, work queues and event pipelines using Redis Streams, and microservices that need a fast shared cache in front of a slower system of record. Startups that want to self-host cheaply, as well as larger organizations that prefer a managed Redis Cloud or Enterprise subscription, are both well served depending on their operational appetite.

Data and ML teams exploring retrieval-augmented generation or recommendation systems can also use Redis's vector search capabilities to store and query embeddings alongside existing cached data, making it a reasonable choice for teams that already run Redis and want to avoid introducing an entirely separate vector database.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Should Avoid It
paragraphs: Redis is not the right primary system of record for teams that need strict relational integrity, complex multi-table joins, or heavyweight ACID transactions across large datasets. A traditional relational database is usually a better fit for that job, with Redis layered in front as a cache rather than a replacement.

Because Redis keeps data in memory, teams with very large datasets that are cost-sensitive about RAM may find it becomes expensive to scale compared to disk-based stores, even with tiering options that move colder data to SSD. Organizations that want a fully hands-off database with no capacity planning at all may also find self-hosted Redis more operationally involved than they'd like.

Teams that are uneasy about the project's licensing history, or that require a specific long-term open-source license guarantee for compliance reasons, should review Redis's current licensing terms and community fork options carefully before standardizing on it, since the terms have changed more than once in recent years.

long_form_blocks.heading: Main Features
paragraphs: At its core, Redis offers a compact set of in-memory data structures, including strings, hashes, lists, sets, sorted sets, bitmaps, and HyperLogLogs, each with atomic operations, so common patterns like counters, queues, and leaderboards can be built with a single command instead of application-level locking.

For durability, Redis supports point-in-time RDB snapshots and append-only file (AOF) logging, and for scale and availability it offers Redis Cluster for sharding and replication, plus Sentinel or Active-Active geo-distribution for failover across regions. Lua scripting and Redis Functions let teams push logic into the server to reduce round trips.

Beyond the core engine, modules add specialized capabilities: RedisJSON for native JSON documents, RediSearch for full-text and vector similarity search, RedisTimeSeries for time-stamped metrics, and RedisBloom for probabilistic structures like Bloom filters. Security features including TLS, ACL-based access control, and, on Redis Cloud, SSO and RBAC round out the platform for production use.

long_form_blocks.heading: The Redis Experience
paragraphs: Day to day, most developers interact with Redis through one of its many official and community client libraries, for Python, Node.js, Java, Go, and other major languages, or through the redis-cli for quick inspection and debugging. The command set is simple and well documented, which keeps the learning curve shallow for anyone who has used a key-value store before.

RedisInsight, Redis's free desktop and browser-based GUI, gives visibility into keys, memory usage, and slow queries without requiring the command line, and the Redis Cloud console adds a management layer for provisioning databases, monitoring throughput, and configuring high availability. Documentation is thorough, and the large community around Redis means most integration questions already have a well-trodden answer.

Where the experience gets more demanding is in self-hosted operations at scale: tuning persistence, planning memory headroom, and configuring clustering correctly require real operational knowledge, and teams that skip this groundwork sometimes hit avoidable outages or memory pressure. Choosing Redis Cloud or Enterprise offloads most of that operational burden in exchange for a usage-based bill.

features.title: In-Memory Data Structures
features.description: Native support for strings, hashes, lists, sets, sorted sets, and more, each with atomic commands so common patterns don't need extra application logic.

features.title: Persistence Options
features.description: RDB snapshots and append-only file (AOF) logging let teams balance performance against durability guarantees.

features.title: Redis Cluster
features.description: Built-in sharding and horizontal scaling across nodes for larger datasets and higher throughput.

features.title: Replication & Sentinel
features.description: Primary-replica replication with Sentinel-managed failover for high availability.

features.title: Active-Active Geo-Distribution
features.description: Multi-region, conflict-free replicated writes available on Redis Enterprise and Redis Cloud Pro.

features.title: Pub/Sub & Streams
features.description: Lightweight publish-subscribe messaging plus Redis Streams for durable, consumer-group-based event logs.

features.title: Search and Vector Similarity
features.description: RediSearch adds full-text search and vector search for AI and recommendation use cases.

features.title: JSON & Time-Series Support
features.description: RedisJSON and RedisTimeSeries extend Redis to document and time-stamped data workloads.

features.title: Scripting & Redis Functions
features.description: Lua scripting and server-side Redis Functions reduce round trips for multi-step operations.

features.title: RedisInsight GUI
features.description: A free visual tool for browsing keys, profiling memory, and diagnosing slow commands.

pros: Extremely low latency for reads and writes thanks to its in-memory design.
Rich, purpose-built data structures reduce the need for application-side workarounds.
Flexible deployment: self-host the open-source engine or use managed Redis Cloud/Enterprise.
Mature ecosystem with client libraries for virtually every major programming language.
Extends beyond caching into search, JSON, time-series, and vector workloads without adding a new system.
cons: As an in-memory store, cost scales with RAM, which can make very large datasets expensive compared to disk-based databases.
Not designed to be the sole system of record for workloads needing complex relational joins or heavy multi-row ACID transactions.
Self-hosted clustering, persistence tuning, and capacity planning require real operational expertise.
Licensing has changed more than once in recent years, leading to community forks such as Valkey and some uncertainty for teams that need a fixed long-term license.
Usage-based Redis Cloud pricing can become costly at scale if memory usage and throughput aren't actively monitored.

faqs.question: Is Redis free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. The core Redis server is open source and free to self-host. Redis Ltd also offers a free tier of its managed Redis Cloud service, alongside paid Essentials, Pro, and Enterprise plans for teams that want a managed or dedicated deployment.

faqs.question: Is Redis a database or a cache?
faqs.answer: Both. Redis is commonly used as a cache in front of a primary database, but its persistence, replication, and clustering features also let teams run it as a primary database for the right workloads, particularly ones built around its native data structures.

faqs.question: What's the difference between Redis and Redis Cloud?
faqs.answer: Redis is the open-source engine you can download and run yourself. Redis Cloud is Redis Ltd's fully managed hosting service, which handles provisioning, scaling, backups, and high availability for a usage-based fee.

faqs.question: Does Redis support data persistence, or is everything lost on restart?
faqs.answer: Redis supports persistence through RDB point-in-time snapshots and append-only file (AOF) logging, so data can be recovered after a restart; teams can tune how aggressively each is used based on their durability needs.

faqs.question: What license does Redis use?
faqs.answer: Redis's licensing has changed more than once in recent years. Teams should check the current license terms on redis.io before adopting it for compliance-sensitive projects, since terms differ between the core engine and various modules.

use_cases.title: Application & API Caching
use_cases.description: Placing Redis in front of a relational or document database to cache frequently read data cuts response times and reduces load on the primary datastore.

use_cases.title: Session Storage & User State
use_cases.description: Storing session tokens, shopping carts, and other short-lived user state in Redis keeps web and mobile applications fast and stateless at the application tier.

use_cases.title: Real-Time Leaderboards & Counters
use_cases.description: Sorted sets and atomic counters make Redis a natural fit for live leaderboards, rate limiting, and usage counters that update on every request.
use_cases.audience: Gaming, social, and consumer product teams

use_cases.title: Messaging, Streams & Event Pipelines
use_cases.description: Pub/Sub and Redis Streams let services publish and consume events with low latency, supporting chat, notifications, and lightweight event-driven architectures.

features: 250 MB to 100 GB of RAM and SSD
SAML SSO and role-based access control
Encryption in transit and at rest
Unlimited RAM on dedicated cloud infrastructure

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Redis is an open-source in-memory data store for caching, databases, and messaging, with usage-based Redis Cloud and enterprise plans.

enrichment.short_description: Global employer-of-record, payroll, and HR platform for hiring and paying employees and contractors abroad.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Remote (Remote.com) is a global employment platform founded in 2019 and headquartered in San Francisco, California. It helps companies hire, pay, and manage employees and contractors in other countries without needing to set up a local legal entity themselves, a process that would otherwise require local legal counsel, tax registrations, and payroll infrastructure in every target market.

The platform bundles employer-of-record (EOR) services, global payroll, contractor management, contractor-of-record, and a broader HR toolkit covering benefits, equity, and compliance into one system. Instead of stitching together local law firms, payroll vendors, and PEOs on a country-by-country basis, teams can manage the whole international employment lifecycle, from first offer letter to eventual offboarding, from a single dashboard.

A key differentiator Remote emphasizes is that it owns many of its own legal entities in the countries it operates in, rather than relying entirely on third-party partners the way some competitors do. Remote positions this as reducing dependency risk and improving support responsiveness for customers hiring across dozens of markets simultaneously, since fewer intermediaries are involved in any single employment relationship. It competes in a crowded global employment category alongside providers such as Deel, Papaya Global, and Oyster, and it markets itself heavily on price transparency, publishing flat monthly fees for its core EOR and contractor products directly on its website rather than gating most pricing behind a sales conversation.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Remote Is For
paragraphs: Remote is built for companies that want to hire full-time employees in countries where they have no legal entity. Startups and scale-ups expanding internationally, whether to access specific talent pools or to support existing customers in new regions, are a core audience, since standing up a foreign subsidiary can take months and significant legal spend.

It also suits HR, People, and finance teams at mid-market and larger companies that need a single system of record for global compliance, payroll, benefits, and equity across many countries, rather than juggling separate vendors, contracts, and invoices per region. Centralizing this administration can simplify reporting and reduce the operational overhead of managing a distributed workforce.

Companies that regularly engage international freelancers or contractors, and want standardized contracts, consolidated payments, and protection against worker misclassification, are another strong fit, especially those using Remote's Contractor Management or Contractor of Record products to formalize what might otherwise be an ad hoc freelance relationship. Founders and operations leads who value pricing transparency during vendor evaluation also tend to gravitate toward Remote, since being able to estimate EOR and contractor costs directly from the website, before engaging sales, makes it easier to compare against other global employment providers early in a buying process.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Should Avoid It
paragraphs: Very early-stage teams hiring only one or two contractors domestically, or businesses hiring exclusively within their own country, may find Remote's per-employee and per-contractor fees unnecessary overhead compared to simpler domestic payroll tools that don't carry a global-employment price premium.

Cash-conscious startups should weigh the EOR fee carefully: at several hundred dollars per employee per month, it can meaningfully affect burn rate for companies hiring many people in lower-cost regions, where the platform fee itself can rival or exceed local statutory employment costs on a percentage basis.

Organizations needing highly specific country coverage that Remote doesn't directly own, or deep custom integrations beyond what the public Remote API currently supports, may need to request a quote or evaluate alternatives before committing, since not every pricing tier or coverage detail is fully self-serve. Companies wanting a low-commitment, try-before-you-buy experience may also be frustrated, since Remote's core EOR and Global Payroll products are not built around a conventional free trial, and larger deployments typically involve a sales-assisted quoting process rather than instant self-checkout.

long_form_blocks.heading: Main Features
paragraphs: At its core, Remote offers Employer of Record services so companies can put a worker on payroll in a country without a local entity, handling local employment contracts, statutory benefits, tax withholding, and terminations in line with each country's specific labor law requirements.

Global Payroll extends this to companies that already have their own foreign entities, consolidating payroll runs across countries into one process, while Contractor Management and Contractor of Record cover independent contractors with localized agreements, invoice approval workflows, and misclassification protection at increasing levels of coverage.

Around these core products sit a broader HR platform with benefits administration, equity management for distributed teams, pre-employment background checks, and a Remote API that lets engineering teams pull employment, payroll, and HR data into internal tools or an existing HR technology stack rather than working solely inside Remote's own interface. Together these features are designed to cover the full arc of international employment, from sourcing and contracting a worker through paying them accurately in local currency, staying compliant with local labor law, and giving them benefits and equity comparable to a domestically hired employee.

long_form_blocks.heading: The Remote Experience
paragraphs: New customers typically start by entering an employee's country and salary details to see estimated employment costs, then move through a guided onboarding flow to draft compliant local contracts and collect the worker's personal and banking information before their agreed start date.

Remote publishes much of its EOR and contractor pricing directly on its website, which is relatively unusual in the EOR space and lets prospective customers estimate costs before ever talking to sales, though Global Payroll and larger enterprise deals still require a custom quote tailored to headcount and country mix.

Day to day, administrators manage employees, contractors, payroll runs, benefits, and compliance tasks from a unified dashboard, with support channels available for questions on local labor law, benefits eligibility, or payment issues, positioning Remote as a single point of contact for ongoing distributed workforce administration rather than a one-time onboarding tool. Overall, the experience is oriented toward operators who want predictable, published pricing and a consolidated platform for global hiring, with the trade-off that very large or highly customized deployments still funnel into a traditional B2B sales cycle for final terms.

features.title: Employer of Record (EOR)
features.description: Hire full-time employees in other countries without setting up a local entity; Remote acts as the legal employer for payroll, tax, and compliance.

features.title: Global Payroll
features.description: Run consolidated payroll across multiple countries for companies that already have their own foreign legal entities.

features.title: Contractor Management
features.description: Create localized contractor agreements, approve invoices, and pay international contractors from one dashboard.

features.title: Contractor of Record
features.description: Remote directly engages and pays contractors on a company's behalf, adding compliance oversight and misclassification protection.

features.title: Global HR Platform (HRIS)
features.description: Centralized records for employees and contractors, including documents, time off, and organizational data across countries.

features.title: Benefits Administration
features.description: Access to locally compliant benefits packages such as health insurance and retirement contributions for employees hired through Remote.

features.title: Equity Management
features.description: Grant and manage stock options or equity compensation for international team members in line with local regulations.

features.title: Remote API
features.description: Developer API for integrating employment, payroll, and HR data into internal tools or existing HR tech stacks.

features.title: Background Checks
features.description: Run pre-employment background checks on candidates in supported countries as part of the hiring workflow.

features.title: Compliance and Local Contracts
features.description: Locally compliant employment and contractor agreements drafted to reflect each country's labor law requirements.

pros: Publishes flat-rate EOR and contractor pricing directly on its website rather than requiring a sales call for every quote
Owns many of its own legal entities in supported countries rather than relying solely on third-party partners
Combines EOR, global payroll, contractor management, and HR tools in a single platform
Contractor plans include misclassification protection and indemnity coverage on higher tiers
Offers a dedicated equity management tool for compensating distributed teams
cons: EOR pricing sits toward the higher end of the market compared to some competitors
No free plan, so per-employee and per-contractor fees apply from the first hire
Global Payroll and enterprise-level pricing are not published and require contacting sales
Costs can add up quickly for companies hiring many employees in lower-cost regions
Deeper integrations with existing HR systems may require custom work via the Remote API

faqs.question: What is Remote.com used for?
faqs.answer: Remote is used to hire, pay, and manage employees and contractors in other countries, handling local compliance, payroll, benefits, and contracts through a single platform.

faqs.question: How much does Remote cost?
faqs.answer: Contractor Management starts at $29 per contractor per month, Contractor Management Plus is $99 per contractor per month, and Employer of Record starts around $599 to $699 per employee per month depending on billing cycle. Global Payroll pricing requires contacting sales.

faqs.question: Does Remote offer a free trial?
faqs.answer: Remote does not advertise a traditional free trial for its EOR or payroll products; instead it publishes estimated pricing upfront and offers demos or quotes for enterprise needs.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Contractor Management and Contractor of Record?
faqs.answer: Contractor Management helps companies create contracts and pay contractors they already engage directly, while Contractor of Record has Remote directly engage and pay the contractor, adding a further layer of compliance protection.

faqs.question: Which countries does Remote support?
faqs.answer: Remote supports employer-of-record and payroll services across a large number of countries worldwide, though exact coverage and local pricing can vary and are best confirmed on Remote's website for a specific location.

use_cases.title: Hiring a full-time employee abroad
use_cases.description: A company wants to hire a full-time employee in a country where it has no legal entity, so it uses Remote's EOR service to handle local employment, payroll, and compliance.
use_cases.audience: Startups and scale-ups expanding into new countries

use_cases.title: Paying international contractors compliantly
use_cases.description: A business regularly works with freelancers abroad and uses Contractor Management to generate localized contracts and consolidate international payments.
use_cases.audience: Companies with distributed freelance or contractor workforces

use_cases.title: Running payroll for an existing foreign entity
use_cases.description: A company that already has its own legal entity in a country uses Remote's Global Payroll to process payroll runs alongside its other markets.
use_cases.audience: Mid-market and enterprise finance and HR teams

use_cases.title: Offering equity and benefits to a global team
use_cases.description: A distributed company wants to grant equity and provide locally compliant benefits to international hires using Remote's equity and benefits tools.
use_cases.audience: Companies building distributed teams with equity compensation

features: Misclassification protection up to $100,000 per contractor

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Remote.com review: pricing, features, and use cases for its global EOR, payroll, and contractor management platform.

enrichment.short_description: AI tool that automatically removes photo backgrounds in seconds, with API, plugins, and apps for creators, teams, and developers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Remove.bg is an AI-powered background removal tool that isolates the subject of a photo from its background in a few seconds, without any manual masking or selection work. It was built by Kaleido AI, a Vienna, Austria-based computer vision startup, and launched the remove.bg product in 2018. Canva acquired Kaleido AI in 2021, and remove.bg now operates as part of the Canva family of products while continuing to run as its own standalone website and API.

The core idea behind remove.bg is speed and simplicity: a user uploads an image, and a neural network trained specifically on foreground-background separation returns a cutout with a transparent background, typically in under five seconds. There is no need to draw a selection, adjust a tolerance slider, or manually clean up edges for the vast majority of everyday photos, which is what separates it from traditional photo-editing background removal workflows.

Beyond the free web tool, remove.bg has grown into a small ecosystem: a REST API for developers, desktop apps for Windows and Mac, mobile apps, and plugins for tools such as Photoshop and Figma. This lets the same underlying model be embedded into e-commerce catalogs, marketing pipelines, and creative tools rather than being limited to one-off manual edits on the website.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Remove.bg Is For
paragraphs: Remove.bg is well suited to e-commerce sellers who need clean, consistent product photos on white or transparent backgrounds at volume. Marketplaces and storefronts often require uniform product imagery, and remove.bg's batch tools and API let a seller process hundreds or thousands of catalog images without hiring a retoucher for each one.

It also fits designers, marketers, and social media managers who need quick cutouts for thumbnails, ads, mockups, and graphics. Because the tool integrates with design software like Photoshop and Figma, creative teams can pull a subject out of a photo without leaving their existing workflow, which saves time on repetitive manual selection work.

Developers and product teams building their own apps are another core audience. The remove.bg API returns processed images programmatically, so companies building photo editing, real estate, ID verification, or virtual try-on features can add background removal as a backend step rather than asking end users to visit a separate website.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Should Avoid It
paragraphs: Hobbyists who only need to remove a background occasionally may find the credit-based pricing unnecessary, since the free tier caps output resolution and the paid credits are geared toward regular or high-volume use. For a single one-off image at full resolution, a free built-in tool in image-editing software may be simpler.

Users working with visually complex images, such as fine hair, semi-transparent objects, motion blur, or busy overlapping subjects, may still need manual touch-up afterward. Remove.bg handles the common cases very well, but like any automated segmentation model it is not perfect on every edge case, so pixel-perfect editorial or fashion retouching work may still require a human retoucher.

Teams that need deep, non-background editing, such as full compositing, color grading, or multi-layer photo manipulation, will outgrow remove.bg quickly since it is purpose-built for one task. Those workflows are better served by a full image editor, with remove.bg used only as one step inside a larger pipeline.

long_form_blocks.heading: Main Features
paragraphs: At its center, remove.bg offers one-click automatic background removal driven by a trained AI model, along with the option to replace the removed background with a solid color, a new image, or a blurred version of the original scene. Fine-tuning tools let users manually erase or restore edges when the automatic result needs a small correction.

For technical use cases, the remove.bg API accepts images and returns a processed cutout for integration into external apps, and desktop apps for Windows and Mac allow drag-and-drop batch processing of many images at once without uploading them one at a time through a browser. Plugins for Photoshop and other design tools bring the same model directly into existing creative workflows.

Output options include multiple resolutions and file formats, with higher resolutions reserved for paid credits, and the download can retain a transparent background for further compositing elsewhere. Mobile apps extend the same background-removal capability to phones, and standard support and help documentation cover integration questions for API users.

long_form_blocks.heading: The Remove.bg Experience
paragraphs: Using remove.bg for the first time is intentionally frictionless: a visitor can drop an image onto the homepage without creating an account and see a processed, background-free preview almost immediately. That immediate payoff is part of why the tool became widely known, since the result is visible before any commitment to sign up or pay.

Once a user wants a full-resolution download, an account and credits come into play, and the experience shifts from a free demo to a metered product. The web interface stays simple through this transition, with clear credit counts and straightforward options to buy a one-time pack or start a monthly subscription depending on expected volume.

For teams integrating through the API or desktop apps, the experience is less about a polished interface and more about reliability and consistent output across many images, which is the priority for e-commerce and automated pipelines. Overall, the product experience favors speed and minimal setup over deep manual control, matching its core promise of one-click background removal.

features.title: One-click AI background removal
features.description: Automatically detects the subject of a photo and removes the background in a few seconds without manual selection.

features.title: Background replacement
features.description: Swap the removed background for a solid color, custom image, or blurred version of the original scene.

features.title: REST API
features.description: Send images programmatically and receive processed cutouts for integration into apps, sites, and internal tools.

features.title: Desktop apps for Windows and Mac
features.description: Drag-and-drop batch processing of many images locally without uploading them one at a time in a browser.

features.title: Design tool plugins
features.description: Plugins for tools such as Photoshop bring background removal directly into existing creative workflows.

features.title: Manual touch-up tools
features.description: Erase or restore parts of an image to correct edges the automatic model did not handle perfectly.

features.title: Mobile apps
features.description: iOS and Android apps let users remove backgrounds from photos directly on a phone.

features.title: Multiple resolution and format downloads
features.description: Export processed images at different resolutions, with higher resolutions available through paid credits.

features.title: Credit-based subscription and pay-as-you-go options
features.description: Choose a monthly credit subscription for regular volume or buy non-expiring credit packs for occasional use.

pros: Produces clean cutouts in seconds with no manual selection required
Free preview lets users test results before paying for a download
API and plugins make it easy to embed into existing tools and pipelines
Handles common product and portrait photos reliably at scale
Available across web, API, desktop, and mobile
cons: Full-resolution downloads require paid credits rather than being free
Complex images with fine detail like hair or transparency can still need manual touch-up
Credit-based pricing can be confusing for users with unpredictable volume
Limited to background removal and does not offer broader photo editing
Free tier resolution is capped, which limits use for print or large-format needs

faqs.question: Is remove.bg free to use?
faqs.answer: Remove.bg offers a free tier that lets you preview a background removal and download one image in low resolution, plus limited free API previews each month. Full-resolution downloads require purchased credits or a subscription.

faqs.question: Who owns remove.bg?
faqs.answer: Remove.bg was created by Kaleido AI, a company based in Vienna, Austria, and it has been part of Canva since Canva acquired Kaleido AI in 2021.

faqs.question: Does remove.bg have an API?
faqs.answer: Yes, remove.bg provides a REST API that developers can use to send images and receive processed background-removed results for integration into their own apps and services.

faqs.question: What image types work best with remove.bg?
faqs.answer: Remove.bg works especially well on people, products, and animals with clear edges against a distinct background. Images with fine detail such as loose hair or transparent objects may need manual correction afterward.

faqs.question: How does remove.bg pricing work?
faqs.answer: Remove.bg uses a credit system where each processed image uses a credit. Users can choose a monthly subscription with a set number of credits or buy pay-as-you-go credit packs that do not expire.

use_cases.title: E-commerce product photography
use_cases.description: Sellers process large batches of product photos into consistent, background-free images for online storefronts and marketplaces.

use_cases.title: Marketing and social media graphics
use_cases.description: Marketers pull subjects out of photos to build ads, thumbnails, and promotional graphics without a full design tool.

use_cases.title: App and product integrations
use_cases.description: Developers call the remove.bg API as a backend step to add automatic background removal to their own applications.

use_cases.title: Design and creative workflows
use_cases.description: Designers use plugins to cut out subjects directly inside tools like Photoshop while building mockups and compositions.

features: 50 free low-resolution preview calls per month via API and apps
Unused credits roll over while subscription stays active
Access to desktop apps and plugins
Unused credits roll over while subscription stays active
Suited to higher-volume catalog or content teams
Good fit for irregular or occasional usage
